frameworks.name: paradigm revision under poorly defined constraints
frameworks.one_line: the 'hard problem' of formulating scientific problems via continual conceptual revision

frameworks.name: optimization over pre-specified objectives
frameworks.one_line: the 'easy problem' AI solves by training flexible algorithms on fixed objectives

frameworks.name: cognitive science of scientists
frameworks.one_line: studying how humans formulate problems to design paradigm-inferring computational agents

frameworks.name: world models
frameworks.one_line: theme-issue framing for natural and artificial intelligence

core_question: How can computational agents move beyond solving pre-specified scientific problems to autonomously formulating and revising scientific paradigms?
key_assumptions: scientific research has two parts: solving a problem and coming up with the problem
current AI optimization handles only the 'easy problem'
the 'hard problem' requires continual conceptual revision under poorly defined constraints
human scientists' cognition is a model for paradigm formation
manipulations: conceptual contrast between optimization-driven discovery and paradigm-formation
proposed agenda of studying scientists' cognition to inform agent design
key_findings: recent AI scientific discoveries reduce to training flexible algorithms on pre-specified optimization problems
this 'easy problem' regime cannot perform conceptual revision required for the 'hard problem'
progress requires cognitive-science study of how scientists formulate and update paradigms
interpretations: new computational agents should automatically infer and update their scientific paradigms
advancing AI-for-science demands bridging cognitive science of scientists with algorithm design
limitations_noted: the hard problem is beyond capacities of current scientific-discovery algorithms
